shortDescription.text: Password stored in plaintext without hashing

fullDescription.text: The addUser function stores the user-supplied password directly in the database document without applying any hashing algorithm. The bcrypt hashing code is commented out. An attacker who gains read access to the database obtains all user passwords in cleartext.

shortDescription.text: Password validated via direct string comparison instead of bcrypt

fullDescription.text: The validateLogin function compares passwords using strict equality (===) rather than bcrypt.compareSync. This confirms passwords are stored and compared in plaintext, making credential theft trivial upon database compromise.

shortDescription.text: Use of deprecated and unmaintained bcrypt-nodejs library

fullDescription.text: The module imports bcrypt-nodejs which has been deprecated since 2017 and contains known vulnerabilities. Even if the commented-out hashing code were re-enabled, it would use an insecure library. The project should migrate to 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs'.

shortDescription.text: MongoDB query built from unsanitized user input enables NoSQL injection

fullDescription.text: The userName parameter received from the HTTP request is passed directly into a MongoDB findOne query without validation or sanitization. An attacker can submit a JSON object (e.g., {"$gt": ""}) as the userName field to bypass authentication or enumerate users.

shortDescription.text: No input validation or sanitization on user registration fields

fullDescription.text: The addUser function accepts userName, firstName, lastName, password, and email parameters directly from the request without any validation for type, length, format, or content. This allows injection of arbitrary data types (objects instead of strings), excessively long values, or malicious content.

shortDescription.text: POST /signup endpoint lacks CSRF token validation

fullDescription.text: The POST /signup route is registered without any CSRF protection middleware. An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits a signup form on behalf of a victim, potentially creating accounts or consuming resources.

message.text: Password is stored in plaintext in the MongoDB document. The bcrypt hashing code on line 29 is commented out. Any database compromise exposes all user credentials.
Password comparison uses direct string equality (fromDB === fromUser) instead of bcrypt.compareSync. This confirms the authentication system relies entirely on plaintext passwords.

The bcrypt-nodejs package is deprecated and unmaintained. Even if hashing were enabled, using this library introduces supply-chain risk and potential cryptographic weaknesses. Migrate to 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs'.

The userName parameter is passed directly into MongoDB findOne() without type checking or sanitization. An attacker can submit {"userName": {"$gt": ""}} to bypass authentication and log in as the first user in the collection.

The addUser function (line 17) accepts userName, firstName, lastName, password, and email from untrusted request input without any type, length, or format validation. This enables injection of arbitrary data types into MongoDB documents.
POST /signup route is registered without CSRF protection middleware. An attacker can forge cross-site signup requests to create accounts or exhaust resources.
